content:
# Romans Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 5 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 5:1 — Sinaiticus variant: "we have peace" (indicative) vs. "let us have peace" (subjunctive)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been justified from faith, we have peace toward the God" | Notes the Sinaiticus subjunctive (echomen) but renders indicatively; "toward" for pros |
| LOGOS | "since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God" | Indicative — declarative statement of fact |
| PNEUMA | "since we have been made right in God's sight by faith, we have peace with God" | Indicative — dynamic expansion of "justified" to "made right in God's sight" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the indicative "we have peace" with LOGOS. The subjunctive (Sinaiticus echomen) is noted in the council apparatus, but the indicative fits Paul's declarative context — he is stating a result of justification, not issuing an exhortation. PNEUMA's "made right in God's sight" expands dikaiothe unnecessarily.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Sinaiticus variant documented in council notes.

---

## 5:2 — "rejoice" vs. "boast" vs. "confidently and joyfully look forward"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we boast upon hope of the glory of the God" | Literal — kauchometha means "boast/exult"; stronger than "rejoice" |
| LOGOS | "we rejoice in hope of the glory of God" | Formal — "rejoice" softens kauchometha |
| PNEUMA | "we confidently and joyfully look forward to sharing God's glory" | Dynamic — expands into a participial phrase; adds "sharing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "rejoice." The verb kauchometha can mean "boast" or "rejoice/exult." In this positive context (hope of glory), "rejoice" is appropriate. EMET's "boast" carries negative connotations in English. PNEUMA's expansion adds "sharing" which is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, though EMET's observation about the boasting theme in Romans is noted (Paul uses kauchaomai both positively and negatively).

---

## 5:3 — "sufferings" vs. "tribulations" vs. "problems and trials"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we also boast in the tribulations" | Literal — thlipsesin, "pressures/tribulations" |
| LOGOS | "we rejoice in our sufferings" | Formal — "sufferings" for thlipsis |
| PNEUMA | "We can rejoice, too, when we run into problems and trials" | Dynamic — "problems and trials" diminishes the weight of thlipsis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "sufferings." Thlipsis denotes serious affliction, not minor inconvenience. PNEUMA's "problems and trials" trivializes the word. EMET's "tribulations" is accurate but archaic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:4 — "character" vs. "proven character" vs. "strength of character"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the endurance [produces] proven character" | Literal — dokimen, "tested/proven quality" |
| LOGOS | "endurance produces character" | Formal — simplified to "character" |
| PNEUMA | "endurance develops strength of character, and character strengthens our confident hope of salvation" | Dynamic — adds "strength of" and "confident hope of salvation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering but acknowledged EMET's precision. Dokime specifically means "proven/tested character" — the quality that emerges from testing, like refined metal. "Character" alone captures this in context. PNEUMA's expansion ("confident hope of salvation") adds content not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:5 — "poured into" vs. "poured out in"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the love of the God has been poured out in the hearts of us" | Literal — ekkechutai en tais kardiais, "poured out in" |
| LOGOS | "God's love has been poured into our hearts" | "Into" rather than "in" — directional |
| PNEUMA | "he has given us the Holy Spirit to fill our hearts with his love" | Dynamic — restructures: Spirit fills hearts with love, rather than love being poured |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "poured into." The preposition en can indicate location ("in") or direction ("into"). "Into" better captures the dynamic act of God's love being actively channeled into believers' hearts. PNEUMA's restructuring reverses the agent — Paul says love is poured, with the Spirit as the means; PNEUMA makes the Spirit the one filling.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:6 — "while we were still weak" vs. "utterly helpless"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "while we were still weak" | Literal — asthenes, "weak/powerless" |
| LOGOS | "while we were still weak" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "When we were utterly helpless" | Dynamic — "utterly helpless" intensifies asthenes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's rendering. "Weak" is accurate — asthenes means lacking strength/power. PNEUMA's "utterly helpless" adds an intensifier not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 5:8 — "shows" vs. "demonstrates" vs. "showed his great love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the God demonstrates the love of himself toward us" | Literal — synistemi, "to commend, demonstrate, prove" (present tense) |
| LOGOS | "God shows his love for us" | Formal — "shows" for synistemi |
| PNEUMA | "God showed his great love for us by sending Christ to die for us" | Dynamic — past tense "showed"; adds "great" and "by sending Christ to die" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Synistemi in present tense means "demonstrates/commends" — God's love is being continuously demonstrated. PNEUMA's past tense ("showed") and additions ("great," "by sending Christ to die") alter the grammar and add content. Note: the cross is already mentioned in the second half of the verse, making PNEUMA's addition redundant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:10 — "reconciled" vs. "friendship restored"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "while being enemies we were reconciled to the God through the death of the Son of him" | Literal — katellagement, "we were reconciled" |
| LOGOS | "while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "since our friendship with God was restored by the death of his Son while we were still his enemies" | Dynamic — "friendship restored" for "reconciled" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Reconciled" is the standard theological term (katallasso) and should be retained. PNEUMA's "friendship restored" domesticates the concept — reconciliation in Paul involves the removal of divine wrath (v.9) and the establishment of peace (v.1), not merely the resumption of friendship.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:11 — "reconciliation" vs. "wonderful new relationship"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "through whom we now received the reconciliation" | Literal — katallage |
| LOGOS | "through whom we have now received reconciliation" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "So now we can rejoice in our wonderful new relationship with God because our Lord Jesus Christ has made us friends of God" | Dynamic — significantly expands; "friends of God" and "wonderful new relationship" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's expansion, while warm and accessible, adds theological content ("friends of God") that comes from a different biblical tradition (James 2:23, John 15:15) rather than this passage. Paul's language here is katallage (reconciliation), not philia (friendship).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:12 — "because all sinned" (eph' ho) — the most debated phrase

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the death spread to all humans, because all sinned" | Literal — eph' ho pantes hemarton; "because" for eph' ho |
| LOGOS | "death spread to all men because all sinned" | Formal equivalent — same interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "Adam's sin brought death, so death spread to everyone, for everyone sinned" | Dynamic — adds "Adam's sin brought death" as causal preface; "for" instead of "because" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The consensus follows the Greek reading "because all sinned" (eph' ho = "because"), not the Latin Vulgate's "in whom" (in quo). PNEUMA's addition of "Adam's sin brought death" is interpretively correct but adds a clause not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The eph' ho interpretation is documented extensively in the council notes.

---

## 5:14 — "type" vs. "symbol/representation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who is a type of the [one] coming" | Literal — typos tou mellontos; "type" preserves the technical typological term |
| LOGOS | "who was a type of the one who was to come" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Now Adam is a symbol, a representation of Christ, who was yet to come" | Dynamic — "symbol, a representation" for typos; explicitly names "Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Type" is the established English theological term for typos and should be retained in a translation of this theologically foundational passage. PNEUMA's double gloss ("symbol, a representation") is unnecessarily expansive, and naming "Christ" removes the veiled reference Paul uses ("the one who was to come").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:15 — "free gift" vs. "gift" vs. "gracious gift"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not as the trespass, so also [is] the gift" | Literal — charisma, "grace-gift" |
| LOGOS | "the free gift is not like the trespass" | Formal — "free gift" for charisma |
| PNEUMA | "there is a great difference between Adam's sin and God's gracious gift" | Dynamic — "gracious gift" for charisma; restructures the comparison |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "free gift." The redundancy of "free gift" is deliberate — it emphasizes the gratuitous nature of charisma (from charis, grace). PNEUMA's "gracious gift" is acceptable but LOGOS's rendering is preferred for consistency throughout the Adam-Christ comparison.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:18 — "one act of righteousness" vs. "Christ's one act of righteousness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "through one act of righteousness [it came] to all humans to justification of life" | Literal — di' henos dikaiomatos |
| LOGOS | "so one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Christ's one act of righteousness brings a right relationship with God and new life for everyone" | Dynamic — adds "Christ's" (not in Greek); "right relationship" for dikaiosis; "new life" for zoe |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul's deliberate structural parallelism (one trespass / one act of righteousness) should be preserved without adding "Christ's" or "Adam's" as explicit subjects — Paul lets the comparison do the naming implicitly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 5:20 — "increase" vs. "came in alongside" — the role of law

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] law came in alongside, that the trespass might increase" | Literal — pareiselthen, "came in alongside/by the side" — the law entered the scene supplementarily |
| LOGOS | "the law came in to increase the trespass" | Formal — simplifies purpose clause |
| PNEUMA | "God's law was given so that all people could see how sinful they were" | Dynamic — adds "God's" and "all people could see" — shifts from purpose (increase trespass) to function (reveal sin) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "came in alongside" captures the supplementary nuance of pareiselthen beautifully — the law was not the main act but entered a story already in progress. However, "came in to increase" is more natural English. PNEUMA changes the purpose from "increase the trespass" to "reveal sin" — a significant theological shift (Paul says the law amplifies sin, not merely reveals it).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "alongside" observation is noted in the apparatus.

---

## 5:20 — "abounded all the more" vs. "super-abounded" vs. "became more abundant"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the grace super-abounded" | Literal — hyperperisseusen, a Pauline compound intensifier |
| LOGOS | "grace abounded all the more" | Formal — paraphrases the compound |
| PNEUMA | "God's wonderful grace became more abundant" | Dynamic — adds "wonderful"; "became more abundant" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "abounded all the more." The rare compound hyperperisseusen (hyper + perisseuo) is Paul's linguistic invention to express grace exceeding sin. "Abounded all the more" captures the comparative excess. EMET's "super-abounded" is a creative attempt but reads awkwardly. PNEUMA's "wonderful" is an addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET too literal / wooden for English | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA too expansive / interpretive | 12 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS or EMET |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA nearly identical | 13 | LOGOS rendering adopted as consensus baseline |
| Theological term at stake | 3 | "Reconciled" vs. "friendship" (v.10); "type" (v.14); kauchaomai (v.2) |
| Textual variant (Sinaiticus) | 1 | v.1 subjunctive — indicative preferred |
| PNEUMA adding content not in Greek | 6 | Consistently rejected |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 5 reveals a growing pattern: PNEUMA's dynamic renderings, while often rhetorically effective, increasingly add theological content not present in the Greek. This is most visible in the Adam-Christ typology (vv.12-21), where PNEUMA repeatedly adds explicit subjects ("Adam's sin," "Christ's act"), interpretive expansions ("wonderful," "great"), and theological reframings ("friendship restored" for "reconciled," "see how sinful" for "increase the trespass").

EMET's contributions are most valuable in this chapter for identifying (1) the Sinaiticus subjunctive variant in v.1, (2) the kauchaomai/boast theme that connects back to 3:27 and 4:2, (3) the supplementary nuance of pareiselthen in v.20, and (4) the rare compound hyperperisseusen. LOGOS continues to provide the most balanced consensus-ready renderings.

The most theologically significant divergence is the eph' ho debate in v.12 ("because all sinned"), where all three translators agree on the Greek reading but PNEUMA adds causal material that prejudges the relationship between Adam's sin and universal death.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
